channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Cornyn’s Defeat Fuels Tensions With President Trump in Senate G.O.P.
item.description: Senators are angry President Trump turned on a respected former leader whom they consider a loyal Republican. Now Mr. Trump faces resistance from his own embittered ranks.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Ilana Panich-Linsman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Cornyn, Republican of Texas, noted in his concession to Ken Paxton on Tuesday that he still had time left in the Senate.

item.title: Paxton’s Texas Victory Opens a New Front in the Battle for the Senate
item.description: Many Democrats and some Republicans said the scandal-plagued Ken Paxton’s victory could turn Texas into a battleground state that will determine Senate control.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton defeated Senator John Cornyn in a runoff for the Republican nomination on Tuesday, setting up a marquee race against James Talarico, the Democrat.

item.title: A Blue Texas May Be More Than a Dream for Democrats
item.description: Ken Paxton’s victory for the Republican nomination and a big shift among Hispanic voters have put a Senate seat within reach.
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Ken Paxton Victory Tests Whether Texas Republicans Can Reunite for November
item.description: Republican voters made a familiar bet, that Texas is conservative enough that any Republican, even the most conservative, will beat a Democrat.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton addressed supporters after winning the Republican nomination for U.S. Senate at a watch party on Tuesday.

item.title: A MAGA Victory in Texas, and the Trump Administration’s New Ebola Plan
item.description: Plus, Iran begins lifting an internet blackout.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Lisa Lerer and Simar Bajaj
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: A supporter celebrating Ken Paxton’s win on Tuesday. Mr. Paxton defeated Senator John Cornyn in the Republican Senate runoff in Texas, setting up a marquee race against James Talarico, the Democratic nominee.

item.title: Residents Watch as Israeli War Planes Circle Over Southern Lebanon
item.description: Israel has intensified its deadly military campaign against Hezbollah, the Iran-backed militant group, in recent days, striking targets across Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: Looking out from a rooftop in Baysarieh as strikes target villages in southern Lebanon on Wednesday.

item.title: Global Supply Shortages Deepen as War Drags On, Risking Jobs and Growth
item.description: After three months, the fallout of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is spreading, with developing countries bearing the brunt of the shortfall.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis, Catie Edmondson, Eshe Nelson, Peter S. Goodman and Suhasini Raj
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Picture Alliance, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar before the war with Iran.

item.title: After 88 Days of Censored News, TV and Chat, Iranians Are Coming Back Online
item.description: The government is letting people connect with the world after a near-total internet shutdown. But not everyone has access, and those who do wonder how long it will last.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Sanam Mahoozi
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: At the Grand Bazaar in Tehran this month. For a quarter of a year, most Iranians were only able to access a “domestic internet” of government-approved apps and websites.

item.title: A Quiet Pacific Village Becomes China’s Security Testing Ground
item.description: When a remote Pacific village asked for help with rowdy youth, the Chinese police arrived with a surveillance system. Then came the backlash.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson, Berry Wang and Adam Ferguson
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Fishing on the shoreline in Honiara on the island of Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands.

item.title: Cubans Deported to Mexico Live a Precarious Existence, Report Finds
item.description: A Human Rights Watch report found that many Cubans deported to Mexico by the Trump administration are living in an “indefinite legal limbo” and struggling to get by.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Eva Marie Uzcategui for The New York Times
item.media:description: More than 4,300 Cubans were deported from the United States to Mexico between President Trump’s 2025 inauguration and March 9, 2026.

item.title: Two of America’s Thorniest Political Issues Are Dividing an Arizona Town
item.description: Marana, Ariz., is used to placid local politics, but the town, in a congressional swing district held by Juan Ciscomani, has been convulsed by two national issues, A.I. and immigration detention.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Cassidy Araiza for The New York Times
item.media:description: A proposed data center in Marana, Ariz., would rise from what’s left of the fallow agricultural land that has not been consumed by tract housing.

item.title: The Battle Over Trump’s Immigration Crackdown Comes to a Newark ICE Facility Parking Lot
item.description: For five days, activists have gathered outside a detention center in New Jersey to denounce conditions inside. Federal officials have rejected those concerns.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ley and Mark Bonamo
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators confronted federal agents on Tuesday outside the Delaney Hall immigration detention center in Newark.

item.title: The Best Restaurants in San Francisco
item.description: From world-class Korean barbecue to Mission-style burritos to quintessential California cuisine, here are our current favorites.
item.dc:creator: Eleanore Park, Brian Gallagher and Tejal Rao
item.category: San Ho Won (San Francisco, Calif, Restaurant)
Daeho Kalbijjim and Beef Soup (San Francisco, Calif, Restaurant)
El Buen Comer (San Francisco, Calif, Restaurant)
HK Lounge Bistro (San Francisco, Calif, Restaurant)
Liholiho Yacht Club (San Francisco, Calif, Restaurant)
Noodle in a Haystack (San Francisco, Calif, Restaurant)
Shuggies Trash Pie & Natural Wine (San Francisco, Calif, Restaurant)
item.media:credit: Albert Law

item.title: The Best Restaurants in Atlanta
item.description: There’s no shortage of Southern virtuosity in the 404, but don’t miss the outstanding Indian, Italian and Thai cooking — or the amazing wings from an unexpected spot.
item.dc:creator: Kim Severson

item.title: The Best Restaurants in Portland, Ore.
item.description: The dining capital of the Pacific Northwest remains one of America’s great culinary cities.
item.dc:creator: Brian Gallagher, Brett Anderson, Melissa Clark and Nikita Richardson

item.title: Crypto Firms and Automakers Are Looking to Open Banks, at Trump’s Urging
item.description: Urged on by the White House and a reduction in regulatory oversight, crypto companies and automakers are among those that have applied for banking charters.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.category: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Gu, the chief executive of Upstart, an online lender seeking a bank charter. “It was just a natural next step for us,” Mr. Gu said.

item.title: ‘60 Minutes’ Journalist Who Accused CBS of Political Meddling Loses Her Deal
item.description: Sharyn Alfonsi, whose segment on a brutal Salvadoran prison was pulled abruptly in December, said that CBS News and its top editor, Bari Weiss, had let her contract expire.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Marla Aufmuth/Getty Images for Texas Conference for Women
item.media:description: Sharyn Alfonsi, a CBS News journalist, said the expiration of her contract sent “a chilling message to the entire newsroom.”

item.title: The $400 Million Showdown Between a Billionaire and a California Mayor
item.description: The owner of the Los Angeles Rams and the City of Inglewood are in a dispute over Hollywood Park and SoFi Stadium, which is about to host World Cup matches.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens, Ken Belson and Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inglewood, Calif., argues that its 2015 development agreement with Hollywood Park, which includes SoFi Stadium, is void because of a subsequent state court decision.

item.title: The Viruses Causing New Outbreaks Are Much Less Familiar to Science
item.description: The types of Ebola and hantavirus panicking officials are very different from the species identified decades ago, raising new questions about how to respond.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Uganda Closes Border With Congo Over Ebola Fears
item.description: Ebola response teams and a few others are exempt and will undergo “strict health screening,” a top Ugandan official said.
item.dc:creator: Musinguzi Blanshe and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.media:credit: Badru Katumba/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Red Cross workers moving the body of someone who may have died from Ebola, in Kampala, Uganda, on Tuesday.

item.title: How Delta Steered Around Airline Industry Chaos
item.description: The carrier has become the country’s most profitable by catering to affluent travelers, but it is facing stiffer competition from United.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines doesn’t plan any drastic changes, said Ed Bastian, who has completed a decade as chief executive.

item.title: Xi Jinping Quit Smoking. China Still Cannot.
item.description: China’s tobacco monopoly has become so financially vital to the government that even its powerful leader has failed to curb the country’s smoking habit.
item.dc:creator: Joy Dong
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrie for The New York Times
item.media:description: China has made slow progress curbing tobacco use or enacting a national indoor smoking ban.

item.title: Why Is TikTok in This Book from 2006?
item.description: For decades, publishers have swapped out cultural references in new editions of books to appeal to younger readers. Fans aren’t always thrilled.
item.dc:creator: Angelina Mazza
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Hunting a ‘Frankenfish’ With a Bow and Arrow
item.description: Invasive snakeheads are proliferating in waterways from New York to Florida. Wildlife officials around the Chesapeake Bay are recommending a solution: high-powered compound bows.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler and Caroline Gutman
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times

item.title: Writing Is Fundamental to How We Think
item.description: A.I. can be a crutch that hurts our ability to think creatively.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Winthrop

item.title: Time Gave Me Back What Prison Took Away
item.description: A formerly incarcerated writer reflects on how the prison system didn’t foster change, but befriending time did.
item.dc:creator: Marvin Wade and Evan Bode

item.title: War. Heat. Cigarettes.
item.description: Updates on stories from around the world.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Daniel Berehulak/The New York Times
item.media:description: In Lebanon.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Slams Trump for Missing His Son’s Wedding
item.description: “It was a small ceremony with about 40 guests, none of whom were his father,” the late-night host said of Donald Trump Jr.’s wedding in the Bahamas over the weekend.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: Teachers Union Urges Schools to Curb A.I. Chatbots and Screen Time
item.description: The American Federation of Teachers recommended “no screens” at all for those in second grade or younger, and no A.I. chatbots for students in elementary school.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Singer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Randi Weingarten, president of the American Federation of Teachers, said on Wednesday that teaching and learning in the earliest grades “should be done without A.I.”

item.title: Go Ask Alice Why Tech Start-Ups Are Spending Big on Hype Videos
item.description: A Mad Hatter and a giant rabbit sit around a table discussing an A.I. start-up. This is normal behavior around the Bay Area these days.
item.dc:creator: Natallie Rocha and Mike Kai Chen

item.title: Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O’Hara Resigns After Investigation
item.description: The chief, Brian O’Hara, began leading the department in 2022 as it reeled in the aftermath of the police killing of George Floyd.
item.dc:creator: Ernesto Londoño and Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: Brian O’Hara at a ceremony for new recruits in January.

item.title: Democrats Refused to Primary Tom DiNapoli for Years. This Year Is Different.
item.description: Thomas DiNapoli, the state comptroller in New York since 2007, now faces his first primary challenge, as two Democrats hope to capitalize on anti-incumbent fervor.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Hiroko Masuike/The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York State comptroller, Thomas DiNapoli, left, is facing his first primary challenge since taking office in 2007. His challengers are Drew Warshaw, center, and Raj Goyle, right.
